purpose: Choose or diagnose storage-engine layout and access-method behavior: row/column, memory/disk, B-Tree, LSM, buffering, WAL, compaction, and amplification.
required_context: primary access patterns: point lookup, range scan, append, update, delete, aggregation
maintenance tolerance: compaction, vacuum, checkpoints, rebuilds
ask_if_missing: Is the workload read/range-heavy, write/append-heavy, analytical scan-heavy, or mixed?
Are latency spikes from compaction/checkpointing acceptable?
assume_if_low_risk: prefer B-Tree-like access for stable low-latency reads and ranges
prefer LSM-like access for high write throughput when compaction can be managed

branches.criteria: range scans, ordered iteration, and predictable point reads dominate
branches.recommendation: favor B-Tree/B+Tree-style indexes and monitor page fragmentation, buffer hit rate, and split/merge churn
favor LSM/SSTable-style storage if compaction, tombstones, read amplification, and Bloom filters are operationally managed

branches.criteria: analytical scans and aggregation over few columns dominate
branches.recommendation: favor column-oriented layout or derived analytical store; reason about compression, vectorized scans, and materialization

branches.criteria: incident involves crash recovery or acknowledged writes
branches.recommendation: trace WAL, dirty page flushing, checkpoint, redo/undo, and force/steal semantics before blaming application code

failure_modes: choosing LSM for writes but ignoring compaction backlog and tombstone read cost
choosing B-Tree for high churn but ignoring fragmentation and vacuum/maintenance
adding secondary indexes without write amplification budget
assuming acknowledged write equals durable write without checking WAL/fsync policy
mixing OLTP and scan-heavy workloads in one cache without isolation
output_checks: states access pattern and layout choice
names maintenance and metrics to watch
